item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a soft spot for math-based skips, and this tickles that soft spot. It’s a bit hard to understand at first, but it’s still pretty cool to think about. Great work.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you would like "Laundry Files".

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Right on. Consider it vetoed.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I don't like this proposed link.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Works for me, but will it work for spikebrennan?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Not sure if the link to 2317 is appropriate, since that article (as far as I can tell or remember) does not involve the Devourer being created from the mind or through mathematical calculations.

At this point, I'm basically advertising my own stuff… but SCP-2698 is about a thing that was conjured into the plane of existence via computer algorithm (albeit as a thing inside a computer).

To convert that to a practical suggestion, I would suggest this:

cerebrum1

Footnotes
1. The manifestation of SCP-2698-A via SCP-2698 is postulated to represent a non-lethal variant of said phenomenon.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Author has vetoed the proposed crosslink.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

As one of the more prominent eldritch horrors on the site, this really is a fun article. How the beast came into the world through someone's mind reminded me a bit of SCP-2317 and how this thing may have made its own portal via the mathematical shenanigans going on in poor Niels Meyer's noggin. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

The point of origin of SCP-1512 is located on the campus of ███████ University. Site-166 has been constructed around SCP-1512 (under the cover story that the facility is part of the university's physical plant) to contain and study it. The tendrils emanate from a specific point of origin, the position of which was formerly the location of a point within Unit 38A of the university's graduate student housing. That dormitory unit had been occupied by Niels Meyer, a PhD candidate in mathematics at the university. Meyer's remains were recovered from the location, and forensic analysis suggests that the point of origin was within Meyer's cerebrum. A notebook recovered from the dormitory unit suggests that Meyer had been studying a hyperbolic non-Euclidean geometric problem when the structure came into being. The containment protocol was developed by Foundation researchers after analyzing the notebook: before the containment protocol was instituted, the structure's growth resulted in Meyer's death and significant structural damage to the dormitory building.

To this:

The point of origin of SCP-1512 is located on the campus of ███████ University. Site-166 has been constructed around SCP-1512 (under the cover story that the facility is part of the university's physical plant) to contain and study it. The tendrils emanate from a specific point of origin, the position of which was formerly the location of a point within Unit 38A of the university's graduate student housing. That dormitory unit had been occupied by Niels Meyer, a PhD candidate in mathematics at the university. Meyer's remains were recovered from the location, and forensic analysis suggests that the point of origin was within Meyer's cerebrum.1 A notebook recovered from the dormitory unit suggests that Meyer had been studying a hyperbolic non-Euclidean geometric problem when the structure came into being. The containment protocol was developed by Foundation researchers after analyzing the notebook: before the containment protocol was instituted, the structure's growth resulted in Meyer's death and significant structural damage to the dormitory building.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Whether this point of origin was a phenomena similar to SCP-2317 or a different kind of anomaly is currently under investigation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fact that humans are perfectly capable of looking at all logical evidence arrayed before them, and yet still disbelieve its existence, meaning, and conclusion, is a very important result in understanding the evolution of cognition.

We have seen the destructive effects that this behavior causes: limits in scientific progress, eradication of biomes, devastation of self-sufficiency, wars over ideological concepts with no objective reality. It has caused humanity untold suffering and stunted development in myriad ways.

Despite all this evidence, we must nonetheless conclude this seemingly maladaptive behavior exists because its benefits outweigh the costs.

Thanks to this behavior, we never have to perceive or believe what, precisely, those benefits are.

ETA: Here's some music to enjoy this SCP by.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And that's how Adeptus Mechanicus was born. Praise the Omnissiah!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When MathBling drives to the wikidot offices & gets all of the wikidot employees hooked on math.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: math proves that .999 repeating = 1, which is obviously hard to believe

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You got my upvote before I even finished reading the containment procedures. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now those are some interesting containment procedures. You gained my upvote around "negative epistemology", and secured it with "requisite level of cognitive dissonance".

Also, I love that this is the team after Omega-7.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So let's recap. We've got Yog-Sothoth's bastard nephew in a University somewhere, that grew literally out of some poor bastard's brain, because he was doing calculations in his head. And the only thing we can do to stop it is to, to put it simply, pray math at it.

Someone get the Laundry on the phone and get ahold of Bob Howard. Or Angleton. Fuck it, get both of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*finger point* Math doesn't exist!

While I understood maybe half of this article at best, I can sort of grasp what it is, and it's creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *coughs conspicuously*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have a look at the Horizon Initiative, good sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. My upvote was secured right there at the end, because the Jesuits are awesome. Heck, a Jesuit GOI would be pretty cool… Militant theological scientists!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Omega-8,the most miserable MTF ever,ever!
Here,your upvote.

item.title: Re: HA HA OH WOW
item.content:encoded: That's just how subtle I am, Dex.

item.title: HA HA OH WOW
item.content:encoded:
I JUST GOT THE NAME.1

ITS A ROOT-SHAPED, INDESTRUCTIBLE ELDRITCH THING THAT SHOULDN'T EXIST IN A RATIONAL UNIVERSE.

AND THE MATH IS AN IRRATIONAL MATH-ROOT AND REQUIRES SOMEONE TO IRRATIONALLY DENY ITS EXISTANCE.

A PUN

Footnotes
1. middle of class it hit me. i opened my browser just to find this article to post this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Incorrect terms there. 1km3 = 1,000m x 1,000m x 1,000m = 1,000,000,000. 80km3 thus is 80,000,000,000, which is a cube about 4,308m per side.

But you are correct in your math. 1 cubic meter of water weighs 1 metric ton. Thus 80,000 metric tons of water takes up 80,000 cubic meters, which is a 43.08m cube, yes. But the volume is .00008km3, not 80.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing with this. Additionally, humans are not completely rational, and as such, can disbelieve something which might be, in fact, logically infallible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're completely missing the point: the people involved in the containment procedure understand, on an intellectual level, that the math is right. But the containment procedure requires them to simultaneously believe, with a religious level of conviction, that the math is _not_ right. Hence, cognitive dissonance: these people basically have to simultaneously believe "X" and "not X".

This is why they get Jesuits and Zen Buddhists to do it for them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll just leave this here:
http://arxiv.org/pdf/1210.8144v1.pdf

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because we might well have tried and it didn't work?
This thing is somehow affected by human cognitive processes. The above is the right set of circumstances to make the processes we want happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, but the implication here is the math is right. And why not use computers? They'll go faster and are more accurate, both of which conditions affect the containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. There's *plenty* of reasons to disbelieve correctly done math.
Just a few: faulty initial assumptions, inadequate model for the task, inaccurate data.

Hell, take stress concentration for example. Do the math for a sharp notch, you'll get a singularity. The result, when processed right is useful, but I don't think anyone ever believed that infinite stress is acting when I tear a piece of paper in half.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Moreover, the procedure has been shown to be ineffective unless the individual performing the calculations simultaneously maintains negative epistemology with respect to the computational work—that is to say, the individual must disbelieve the results of the mathematical computations.

Here's the great thing about math. You can't disbelieve it. It doesn't matter what you say, as long as the math is performed correctly, you have no choice but to believe the results.

Have my downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I — wha — it — what the hell did I just read?

This is brilliant and very, very strange. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How soon 'til we can upvote comments? >.<

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some articles are born good, and some must be beaten with wire hangers and forced to be good.
i would venture that more effort has gone into saving this idea than any three other randomly choosen from the list, and the result is all the more impressive for knowing the long slog to get here.

Well done, sir/madam, well done indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Math - Not even once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The old version of this was interesting, but had a few elements that were just…off. This version is just right. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can finally upvote this. Great job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I shall mourn the submarine's loss, but I approve of its replacement. Hopefully we won't be seeing this deleted and rewritten again, yes? :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it has an unmeasurably high Vickers hardness and has proven immune to damage by cutting, burning, lasers, corrosive acids and similar substances, and other means

As soon as I saw this, I realized that it was a successful rewrite of the submarine and that the fact that this was pulled off deserved my upvote.

I'm just sad there are no Centikavas :c

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They should just build a calculus lecture hall around it.

There will be plenty of people that are capable of complex calculations and don't believe a single thing they get.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love that you finally nailed the execution on this. All of my +1's, and I should add that the imagery here is brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it when you talk down to me Vae, especially on other people's articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it the first time, like it even more now! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a reality bender.

This is a reality bender on math.

Remember kids-don't do math.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That phrase made my day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And well done at that.
*slaps lynx seal of approval on*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My use of dry, technical tone here is very deliberate. This is the Foundation confronting some kind of squamous, rugose Lovecraftian horror fron an incomprehensible non-Euclidean dimension of space-time… as an engineering problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It can't be Euclid; it's the opposite of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's actually exactly what this is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It almost looks like it's "Fight Yog-Soggoth with math, while disbelieving in said math."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The way I looked at it, 80K metric tons is in between the displacement of the Titanic and that of a Nimitz-class aircraft carrier (and keep in mind that displacement doesn't describe the size of the ship but only the part below the waterline). Further, given the hardness of the thing, I wouldn't assume a density equal to that of water.

A large building-sized containment facility ought to do… for now …

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is probably the most drastic rewrite I've ever seen. Well done. I like that the containment procedures are basically "Fight math with more math".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now.
First of all, maintaining a negative epistemology is explained in the same bloody sentence it's mentioned in.

Second.. as far as I know, the topology thing is one of those math areas that normally don't correspond to anything we might find in reality, which gives out just how fucked up this is.

Third thing, wikipedia.

Fourth thing - the way this is written adds to its believability. If the article is going to be written in a way resembling Simple English wikipedia, it's not going to be believable as an internal document passed among experts with an explicit need to know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, I really like the story you set up, the tendrils coming out of the math student's head, the constant equations being conducted by the MTF, the idea that as soon as you acknowledge what the truth of the numbers is this giant tentacle appears- very lovecraftian.

But for the love of all things holy, please revise the second paragraph of the containment procedures into something more intelligible. What on earth does Riemannian mean? Is "maintain a negative epistemology" really the best way to express the thought? I think it would really improve the article if you had fewer ten-dollar words and a bit more of a straightforward explanation of the math involved, at least for laypeople like myself. Obviously a successful article, but would like to have those terms more clearly explained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I had to reread it over a couple times as well, my brain just doesn't work well with things like that, but eventually I got it and it made the article much clearer, but I liked it for the imagery and the weirdness of the tentacleage well before that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was quite clear for me… after the third read. This is an extremely spot on and unique take on the universe of maths. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In order to conduct Procedure 1512-Alpha, the on-duty task force members must compute, manually and in real time, the differential pseudo-Riemannian topology of the asymptotes of SCP-1512's point of origin.

No vote because I have absolutely no idea what's going on; everytime I try to read past that sentence, my eyes cross. Then again, all maths does that to me and I guessed from the comments this has something to do with maths…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
thank god, I thought I'd dreamed it to. Didn't it used to be some Cthullu like thing sprouting from a derelict submarine?
But, yes, I love this.
+1

That's an idea! A text you read and are certain you've read before….even though that's patently impossible….hmm….

Or—something that inspires Deja Vu or Jamais Vu in its vicinity….hmm….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hang a mo, wasn't this pretty much SCP-1844? Or am I going insane?

EDIT: SCP-1844 seems to have been deleted. Did you decide to redo it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The disbelief trick seems to come from the notes of said faculty member.
That said, I think they estimated the mass by its impact on surroundings somehow - i'm not an expert on soil mechanics but at this sort of mass it should become possible to get a ballpark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautifully bizzaro imagery and containment requirements. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much improved, but still has some major fridge logic. How did the Foundation get to this before half the city saw it? How do they measure this thing's mass? How did they figure out the disbelief trick?
